title: E-commerce Price Monitoring
cardTeaser: Stopped checking prices manually — now gets Slack alerts before breakfast.
summary: A Texas e-commerce brand was getting undercut overnight with no visibility until customers compared prices on Google.
challenge: Ops spent 3 hours every morning copying competitor prices into Excel and still missed mid-day changes.
solution: Nightly scraper across 12 stores with Slack alerts when any price drops more than 5%.
results: Reaction time went from days to hours. Paid for itself in week one.
context: A Texas-based Shopify brand selling home goods competed against a dozen similar stores. Pricing changed daily — sometimes hourly during sales seasons — and their ops lead spent every morning manually checking competitor sites.
By the time spreadsheets were updated, prices had already shifted again. They were reacting days late, losing margin on flash sales and missing chances to undercut rivals on slow movers.
challengeParagraphs: Manual tracking could not scale across 12 stores × 200 SKUs. Human error meant wrong prices in decision decks.
solutionParagraphs: Automated nightly scrape with intraday re-runs during Black Friday. Alerts include old price, new price, and direct link.
resultsParagraphs: Reaction time collapsed from days to under an hour. Team repriced strategically and recovered margin on two key categories.

painPoints.label: 3+ hours every morning on manual price checks
Margin leaks from reacting days too late
Mid-day price drops completely missed
Excel workflow nobody trusted

approach: Scoped 12 competitor storefronts — confirmed public product pages only.
Mapped SKU fields needed: price, compare-at, stock badge, title hash.
Built Python scrapers with rotating residential proxies.
Normalized data into PostgreSQL with change-detection triggers.
Slack webhook alerts when any SKU drops more than 5%.
Daily CSV export for merchandising team legacy workflow.
Dashboard for ops lead — filter by category, sort by biggest delta.
Two-week parallel run vs manual checks to validate accuracy.
Documented runbook for adding new competitors.
Handoff: cron on Render, env vars, on-call fix SLA.

timeline.detail: Legal review, target list, field mapping, alert rules.
Scrapers, database, diff engine, Slack integration.
Parallel manual comparison, tuning, production deploy.

outcomes.label: Weekly time saved
outcomes.detail: Ops lead redirected to strategy work.

outcomes.label: Reaction time
outcomes.detail: Down from 2–3 days on average.

outcomes.label: ROI
outcomes.detail: Tool paid for itself after first prevented undercut.

deepSections.title: The manual pricing trap
paragraphs: Every morning the ops lead opened 12 tabs — one per competitor — and copied prices into a shared Excel file. Two hundred SKUs across a dozen stores meant the spreadsheet was stale before lunch.

During Black Friday one rival dropped prices on slow movers at 2 PM. The team did not see it until Monday. They lost a full weekend of margin on two key categories because reaction time was measured in days, not hours.
Enterprise pricing tools quoted $2k+/month. They needed something lean that plugged into Slack — where the team already lived.

deepSections.title: What we built & how it works
paragraphs: We scoped every competitor storefront, confirmed only public product pages were accessed, and mapped the exact fields the merchandising team needed: price, compare-at, stock badge, and a title hash for deduplication.

Python scrapers run on a Render cron — nightly by default, hourly during peak sale windows. Data lands in PostgreSQL with change-detection triggers. When a SKU moves more than 5%, a Slack webhook fires with old price, new price, and a direct link.
We ran a two-week parallel comparison against manual checks to validate accuracy, then handed off a runbook for adding new competitors without developer help.

deepSections.title: Results after launch
paragraphs: Reaction time collapsed from 2–3 days to under an hour. The ops lead reclaimed 15 hours per week and redirected that time to strategic repricing.
The tool paid for itself in week one when an alert caught a competitor undercut on a hero SKU — the team matched within the hour instead of discovering it days later on a customer complaint.

deliverables.title: Multi-store scraper fleet
deliverables.detail: 12 competitor storefronts scraped nightly with residential proxy rotation and retry logic.

deliverables.title: Price history warehouse
deliverables.detail: PostgreSQL with change-detection triggers — every price move logged with timestamp.

deliverables.title: Slack alert pipeline
deliverables.detail: Instant notifications when any SKU drops more than 5%, with old/new price and direct link.

deliverables.title: Ops dashboard
deliverables.detail: Filter by category, sort by biggest delta, export CSV for legacy workflows.

title: Restaurant Website Redesign
cardTeaser: PDF menu and no click-to-call → 40% more phone orders in month one.
summary: A family Italian restaurant had a 2016 site that fell apart on phones where 80% of searches happen.
challenge: Menu was a PDF. Hours buried in footer. Customers said the site looked closed.
solution: Mobile-first rebuild with scrollable menu, sticky call button, and sub-2s load on 4G.
results: Phone orders jumped 40%. Three Google reviews praised the easy new site.
context: A family-run Italian restaurant in Ohio had built a loyal local following over 15 years — but their digital front door told a different story. The website was built in 2016, never updated, and on mobile it looked abandoned.

The owner knew most new customers found them on Google from their phones. Yet the menu was a PDF that required pinch-zoom, hours were buried in the footer, and there was no obvious way to call or order. Staff reported hearing "I thought you were closed" several times a week.
They did not need a fancy rebrand — they needed a site that matched the warmth of the dining room and made calling or visiting effortless on a 5-inch screen.
challengeParagraphs: The previous site was a generic WordPress theme with a stock photo of pasta that did not match their actual dishes. On mobile the navigation collapsed into a hamburger menu that hid the phone number entirely.
The PDF menu downloaded instead of displaying inline — many customers on cellular data never opened it. Competitors two blocks away had modern sites with online ordering widgets.
solutionParagraphs: We rebuilt from scratch with a mobile-first layout: hero showing today's hours, one-tap call button fixed at the bottom, and a menu organized by category with real dish photos the owner provided.
We added structured data for local SEO, compressed all images to WebP, and wrote copy in plain language — no "culinary journey" fluff, just what customers need to decide to visit tonight.
resultsParagraphs: Phone orders from mobile increased 40% in the first month — the owner attributed this directly to the sticky call button and clearer hours.
Staff stopped answering "are you open?" questions as often because hours and holiday closures were visible immediately. The project paid for itself within six weeks of additional cover turns.

painPoints.label: 80% of searches on mobile — site unusable there
Menu locked inside a PDF customers hated
Outdated design made the restaurant look closed
No click-to-call — customers gave up and picked another spot

approach: Discovery call — walked through the old site on real phones and listed every friction point.
Content audit — collected menu items, hours, photos, and brand colors from the owner.
Mobile-first wireframes — sticky call button, scrollable menu, hero with same-day hours.
Design mockups — warm photography, readable typography, trust cues (years open, neighborhood).
Development — Astro + Tailwind, image optimization, local SEO basics baked in.
Menu build — categorized dishes with prices, dietary tags, no PDF anywhere.
Speed pass — compressed images, lazy loading, sub-2s target on 4G.
QA on 6 real devices — iPhone SE, Android mid-range, iPad, slow 3G throttling.
Launch + handoff — domain DNS, SSL, simple guide to update specials via spreadsheet.
Week-one check-in — Google Analytics review and small copy tweaks from staff feedback.

timeline.detail: Site audit, competitor scan, content collection, signed-off sitemap.
Mockups for home, menu, contact. Two revision rounds with owner.
Development, menu entry, forms, analytics, mobile QA.
DNS cutover, staff training, post-launch monitoring.

outcomes.label: Phone orders
outcomes.detail: Measured month-over-month from call tracking.

outcomes.label: New Google reviews
outcomes.detail: Mentioned the easy new website specifically.

outcomes.label: Mobile load time
outcomes.detail: Down from 8+ seconds on old WordPress theme.

outcomes.label: PDF menus
outcomes.detail: Fully native scrollable menu — staff can update specials.

deepSections.title: Page 1 — Where they started
paragraphs: When we first opened the site on an iPhone SE, the owner winced. The hero image was pixelated, the phone number was missing above the fold, and the only menu link downloaded a 4MB PDF.

Google Search Console showed 82% mobile traffic but bounce rate on mobile was 71% — nearly double desktop. Customers who did call often said they struggled to find hours or thought the business might be closed.
The owner had tried a cheap template refresh in 2019 but never maintained it. Staff still printed paper menus for anyone who asked because they knew the website failed on phones.

deepSections.title: Page 2 — What we changed
paragraphs: We rebuilt the information architecture around three jobs: confirm you are open, show the menu, make calling easy. Everything else moved down or off the homepage.

Photography came from the owner’s phone — real dishes, real dining room — compressed to WebP with responsive sizes. We removed WordPress entirely in favour of a static Astro build hosted on their existing domain.
Local SEO was part of delivery: Google Business Profile hours synced, schema markup for Restaurant, and page titles rewritten for “Italian restaurant + city” searches instead of generic taglines.

deepSections.title: Page 3 — Results & lessons
paragraphs: Within 30 days, call tracking showed a 40% lift in phone orders originating from mobile. Three new Google reviews explicitly mentioned how easy the site was to use — the first time that happened.
Load time dropped from 8.2s to 2.1s on throttled 4G. The owner updates weekly specials through a simple shared spreadsheet we wired to the menu — no developer needed for routine changes.
The biggest lesson: for local restaurants, the website is not marketing fluff — it is the front door. Speed, hours, and one-tap call matter more than animation or trendy design.

deliverables.title: Mobile-first restaurant site
deliverables.detail: Astro + Tailwind rebuild with sticky call button and scrollable menu.

deliverables.title: Local SEO package
deliverables.detail: Schema markup, Google Business Profile sync, and city-targeted page titles.

deliverables.title: One-tap call CTA
deliverables.detail: Fixed bottom call button visible on every page — drove 40% more phone orders.

deliverables.title: Spreadsheet menu updates
deliverables.detail: Owner updates weekly specials via shared sheet — no developer needed.

title: Lead Generation Bot
cardTeaser: Leads waited 4 hours for a reply. Bot responds in 90 seconds, 24/7.
summary: A 6-person agency lost warm leads after 6 PM when nobody was checking the inbox.
challenge: Generic chat widget dumped emails into a spreadsheet checked only in the morning.
solution: Telegram bot with 4 qualifying questions, lead scoring, and HubSpot push with full transcript.
results: Response time: 4 hours → 90 seconds. Qualified conversions up 28% in Q1.
context: A London marketing agency lost warm leads after 6 PM. Their website chat widget collected names and emails into a spreadsheet nobody checked until the next morning.
Sales reps started each day triaging overnight form fills — by then prospects had already booked calls with faster competitors. Average first response time was four hours.
They needed a bot that qualifies leads instantly, scores them against their playbook, and pushes hot prospects to HubSpot before the conversation goes cold.
challengeParagraphs: Generic widget created noise, not pipeline. Sales checked leads once daily.
solutionParagraphs: Structured bot conversation with scoring and instant CRM push.
resultsParagraphs: Response time 4h → 90s. Sales focuses on warm, scored leads only.

painPoints.label: 4-hour average response time
Leads went cold overnight
No qualification before handoff
Generic widget created noise, not pipeline

approach: Mapped 4 qualifying questions directly from the sales playbook.
Built Telegram bot with branching logic and lead scoring weights.
HubSpot integration — full chat transcript attached to contact record.
Escalation path to human rep for high-value leads scoring above threshold.
Weekly drop-off report showing where prospects abandon the flow.
A/B tested opening message to improve conversation start rate.
Documented playbook updates so marketing can tweak questions without code.

timeline.detail: Sales script audit, CRM field mapping, qualification criteria.
Bot logic, scoring engine, HubSpot integration, escalation rules.
Go-live, first-week tuning, drop-off analysis.

outcomes.label: First reply
outcomes.detail: Down from 4 hours average.

outcomes.label: More qualified leads
outcomes.detail: Q1 after launch vs same period prior year.

outcomes.label: Lead coverage
outcomes.detail: Evenings and weekends no longer a dead zone.

deepSections.title: Leads dying after business hours
paragraphs: The agency's chat widget looked modern but behaved like a contact form. Submissions landed in a shared spreadsheet. Sales checked it once each morning — four hours after the first overnight lead arrived.

Prospects who filled the form at 7 PM had often already booked discovery calls with competitors by 9 AM. The widget created volume but not pipeline — no budget questions, no timeline, no way to separate tire-kickers from ready buyers.

deepSections.title: Structured bot with sales-grade qualification
paragraphs: We extracted four qualifying questions directly from the sales playbook: budget range, timeline, team size, and current tooling. Each answer maps to a weighted score stored in PostgreSQL.

The Telegram bot runs 24/7 with branching logic — follow-up questions adapt based on earlier answers. Every conversation syncs to HubSpot with the full transcript attached. Leads scoring above threshold trigger an instant escalation to the on-call rep.
Marketing can update question wording through a simple config file — no developer needed for playbook tweaks.

deepSections.title: Pipeline impact
paragraphs: First response time dropped from four hours to 90 seconds. Sales stopped wasting mornings on unqualified form fills and focused on scored, warm leads only.
Qualified conversion rate rose 28% in Q1 after launch. The bot handles evenings and weekends — times that were previously a complete dead zone for the agency.

deliverables.title: Telegram qualification bot
deliverables.detail: Branching conversation with 4 playbook questions and automatic lead scoring.

deliverables.title: HubSpot CRM sync
deliverables.detail: Full chat transcript, score, and source attached to every contact record.

deliverables.title: Hot-lead escalation
deliverables.detail: High-score prospects routed instantly to on-call sales rep via Telegram.

deliverables.title: Drop-off analytics
deliverables.detail: Weekly report showing where prospects abandon — used to tune messaging.

title: Competitor Analysis Report
cardTeaser: Founder tracked 8 rivals manually. Now gets a Monday PDF with pricing + feature changes.
summary: Seed-stage founder needed competitor intel but could not afford enterprise CI tools.
challenge: Every Sunday, 2 hours screenshotting competitor pages — mid-week updates missed.
solution: Weekly scrapers on 8 competitors compiled into a branded PDF every Monday 8 AM.
results: Used data to reposition pricing and win 2 enterprise deals.
context: A seed-stage B2B SaaS founder tracked 8 competitors manually every Sunday — screenshotting pricing pages and feature lists into a Google Doc for Monday board prep.
Mid-week pricing changes and feature launches went completely invisible. Enterprise CI tools like Klue and Crayon quoted $2k+/month — out of reach pre-seed.
They needed a lean weekly intelligence pipeline: automated scraping, a branded PDF report,  every Monday at 8 AM, and an archive for board meetings.
challengeParagraphs: Enterprise CI tools cost $2k+/mo — out of reach pre-seed.
solutionParagraphs: Custom weekly pipeline at fraction of cost.
resultsParagraphs: Used report to adjust pricing — won two enterprise pilots.

painPoints.label: 2 hrs every Sunday screenshotting
Missed mid-week pricing moves
Enterprise CI tools too expensive pre-seed
Google Doc intel nobody trusted

approach: Identified 8 competitor pricing and feature pages to monitor.
Built Playwright scrapers with weekly cron schedule.
Designed branded PDF template with week-over-week diffs highlighted.
Email delivery every Monday 8 AM to founder + board distribution list.
Archive folder in Google Drive for quarterly board prep.
Diff engine flags pricing changes, new features, and removed tiers.

timeline.detail: Scrapers, PDF template, diff engine.
Scheduled delivery, first board-ready report.

outcomes.label: Enterprise deals
outcomes.detail: Repositioning informed by weekly intel.

outcomes.label: Sunday hours
outcomes.detail: Founder reclaimed manual screenshot time.

deepSections.title: Flying blind between board meetings
paragraphs: Every Sunday the founder spent two hours screenshotting competitor pricing pages into a Google Doc. By Wednesday those screenshots were already stale — a rival had launched a new tier or dropped enterprise pricing.
Enterprise competitive intelligence platforms quoted $2k+/month. At seed stage that was not an option. The founder needed something custom, cheap, and reliable enough for Monday board prep.

deepSections.title: Automated weekly intelligence pipeline
paragraphs: We built Playwright scrapers for 8 competitor pricing and feature pages, scheduled on a weekly cron. A diff engine compares each run against the previous week and highlights changes — new tiers, price moves, removed features.
Results render into a branded PDF template and email every Monday at 8 AM. An archive folder in Google Drive keeps every report for quarterly board meetings.

deepSections.title: From guesses to data-driven positioning
paragraphs: The founder used the second weekly report to adjust enterprise pricing — repositioning against a rival who had quietly dropped their mid-tier plan. That insight contributed to winning two enterprise pilots in the following quarter.
Sunday screenshot sessions are gone. Board meetings now start with data, not guesses.

deliverables.title: Competitor scraper pipeline
deliverables.detail: 8 rivals monitored weekly — pricing pages, feature lists, and tier changes.

deliverables.title: Branded weekly PDF
deliverables.detail: Auto-generated report with week-over-week diffs, delivered Monday 8 AM.

deliverables.title: Diff highlight engine
deliverables.detail: Flags pricing moves, new features, and removed tiers in red/green.

deliverables.title: Board archive
deliverables.detail: Google Drive folder with every report — ready for quarterly board prep.

title: Offline-First Mobile App
cardTeaser: Technicians in dead zones. App works offline, syncs when signal returns.
summary: Field techs lost job data when connectivity dropped — they went back to paper forms.
challenge: Existing app crashed without internet. Office staff re-typed everything.
solution: Offline-first Android with SQLite, photos, signatures, and background sync.
results: Zero lost jobs after launch. 30% more jobs completed per day.
context: A field services company with 120+ technicians across rural Maharashtra and Karnataka relied on a cloud-only Android app for job assignments, site photos, and customer signatures.

Coverage was patchy — cell towers sparse between villages and inside industrial compounds. When signal dropped mid-job, the legacy app showed a blank error screen and technicians reverted to paper forms.
Office staff spent evenings re-keying the same data into the ERP. The operations head wanted one app technicians trust in dead zones — complete the job offline, sync automatically when back online.
challengeParagraphs: Legacy app required constant connectivity. Technicians reverted to paper. Office staff re-typed everything nightly.
solutionParagraphs: Offline-first React Native app with SQLite, queued uploads, and background sync.
resultsParagraphs: Zero lost jobs after launch. Paper forms eliminated. 30% more jobs completed per day.

painPoints.label: App crashed or froze without internet
Double data entry — field paper, then office ERP
Photos and signatures lost when upload failed
Technicians drove to town just to get signal

approach: Field research — rode along with 3 technicians to map real offline failure points.
SQLite local store — full job package cached on device including checklists and parts.
Offline photo capture — compress, queue locally, batch upload when connected.
Canvas signature pad — stored as PNG in local DB, synced with job record.
Background sync queue with exponential backoff and conflict resolution rules.
Offline map tiles cached per service region — no Google API calls in dead zones.
Push notifications via FCM when sync completes or conflicts need review.
Admin dashboard — sync status per technician, conflict queue, failed upload retry.
Pilot with 10 technicians in worst-coverage zones before full rollout.
Training videos in Hindi and Kannada — 5-minute walkthrough per feature.

timeline.detail: Field ride-alongs, failure mapping, architecture design.
Core offline flows — jobs, photos, signatures, local SQLite.
10 technicians in low-coverage zones, bug fixes, sync tuning.
Full team deployment, training, admin dashboard handoff.

outcomes.label: Jobs per day
outcomes.detail: Less admin time and no signal-hunting drives.

outcomes.label: Lost jobs
outcomes.detail: Zero data loss after launch across 120+ technicians.

outcomes.label: Paper forms eliminated
outcomes.detail: Office re-keying workflow retired within 2 weeks.

deepSections.title: Where field operations broke down
paragraphs: Technicians in rural Maharashtra and Karnataka often worked inside factory compounds and between villages where 4G was unreliable. The legacy app required constant connectivity — when signal dropped, the screen went blank and in-progress job data vanished.

Technicians learned to carry paper forms as backup. They photographed completed forms with their personal phones, drove to the nearest town with signal, and tried to upload. Half the time uploads failed silently. Office staff re-typed everything into the ERP every evening — the same data, entered twice.
The operations head estimated 25% of technician time was lost to connectivity workarounds. Job completion rates plateaued because teams refused to trust the app in dead zones.

deepSections.title: Offline-first architecture we shipped
paragraphs: We started with field ride-alongs — three days shadowing technicians in the worst-coverage zones to map every failure point. That informed an offline-first React Native architecture with SQLite as the local source of truth.

Every job assignment, checklist item, parts entry, photo, and signature lives on-device first. A background sync queue pushes changes when connectivity returns, with exponential backoff and documented conflict resolution rules — server wins on status, client wins on photos and signatures.

Offline map tiles are pre-cached per service region so technicians navigate without API calls. An admin dashboard shows sync status per technician, surfaces conflicts for review, and retries failed uploads automatically.

deepSections.title: Pilot, rollout & measurable impact
paragraphs: We piloted with 10 technicians in the lowest-coverage zones for two weeks — fixing sync edge cases before full rollout. Training videos in Hindi and Kannada covered each feature in under five minutes.

After launch across 120+ technicians: zero lost jobs, paper forms eliminated within two weeks, and 30% more jobs completed per day because admin time and signal-hunting drives disappeared. Technicians now start jobs confidently knowing the app works whether or not they have bars.

deliverables.title: Offline SQLite job store
deliverables.detail: Full job package on device — assignments, checklists, parts used, and status updates.

deliverables.title: Queued photo capture
deliverables.detail: Compress and store locally; batch upload when connectivity returns.

deliverables.title: Offline signature pad
deliverables.detail: Customer sign-off saved as PNG in local DB, synced with job record.

deliverables.title: Background sync engine
deliverables.detail: Exponential backoff, conflict resolution rules, and FCM push on completion.

deliverables.title: Cached offline maps
deliverables.detail: Region tiles pre-downloaded — navigation works with zero signal.

deliverables.title: Admin sync dashboard
deliverables.detail: Per-technician sync status, conflict queue, and failed upload retry.

title: Dental Clinic Modernization
cardTeaser: Old site looked sketchy. Online bookings = 35% of new patients.
summary: Decent local reputation but a 2009-looking site made new patients hesitate.
challenge: No online booking. Missing doctor profiles. Insurance info unreadable.
solution: Trust-first design with doctor photos, insurance logos, and integrated booking.
results: 35% of new patients book online. First time patients complimented the site.
context: A dental clinic in New Jersey had excellent chair-side reputation but a website that looked like 2009 — stock clipart, tiny text, no mobile booking.
New patients googling "dentist near me" bounced within seconds. Front desk said callers often asked "is this the right office?" because the site did not match the modern practice inside.
challengeParagraphs: Competing clinics had modern sites with instant booking. Theirs felt like a placeholder and hurt first impressions on intake calls.
solutionParagraphs: Trust-first redesign with real team photography, insurance clarity, and embedded scheduling. Every page answers "why choose us" in 5 seconds.
resultsParagraphs: 35% of new patients now book online. Front desk handles fewer routine scheduling calls and more complex care questions.

painPoints.label: Site credibility did not match in-office experience
No online booking — phone tag for appointments
Doctor profiles missing — patients could not choose
Insurance info unreadable on mobile

approach: Patient journey mapping — from Google search to booked appointment.
Photography direction — real team photos, not stock smiles.
Trust blocks — insurance logos, credentials, before/after gallery (with consent).
Service pages per treatment with plain-English explanations.
Integrated booking widget with availability sync.
HIPAA-aware contact forms — no PHI in email subjects.
Speed + accessibility pass — WCAG AA contrast targets.
Staff training video — 8 minutes on updating hours and closures.

timeline.detail: Competitor audit, patient interview notes, sitemap.
Trust-first UI, mobile prototypes, content writing.
Development, booking integration, QA, go-live.

outcomes.label: New patients online
outcomes.detail: Book without calling — first time ever.

outcomes.label: Phone volume
outcomes.detail: Routine booking moved self-serve.

outcomes.label: Compliments in reviews
outcomes.detail: Mentioned website specifically.

deepSections.title: First impression was failing patients
paragraphs: Competing clinics two miles away had modern sites with instant booking. This practice had stock clipart, a contact form that looked unencrypted, and insurance information buried in a PDF.

Front desk staff reported that new patients often called to confirm they had the right office — the website simply did not reflect the quality of care inside. Google Analytics showed 78% mobile traffic with a 68% bounce rate.

deepSections.title: Trust-first redesign approach
paragraphs: We mapped the patient journey from Google search to booked appointment and rebuilt every touchpoint around three questions: Can I trust this practice? Do they take my insurance? How do I book?

Real team photography replaced stock images. Each of four doctors got a profile with credentials, specialties, and bar admissions. Service pages explained treatments in plain English — no jargon walls.
We integrated Calendly for self-serve booking, added HIPAA-aware contact forms, and passed WCAG AA contrast targets on every page.

deepSections.title: Impact on intake & front desk
paragraphs: 35% of new patients now book online — the first time the practice offered self-serve scheduling. Routine booking calls dropped 28%, freeing front desk staff for complex care questions.
Six Google reviews in the first quarter specifically mentioned the website. Patients said it finally matched the experience they had in the chair.

deliverables.title: Trust-first redesign
deliverables.detail: Mobile-first site with real team photography, insurance clarity, and credential blocks.

deliverables.title: Local SEO foundation
deliverables.detail: Schema markup, Google Business Profile sync, and treatment-specific landing pages.

deliverables.title: Online booking integration
deliverables.detail: Calendly embed with availability sync — patients book without calling.

deliverables.title: HIPAA-aware forms
deliverables.detail: Encrypted contact intake with no PHI in email subjects or notification previews.

title: Amazon Product Scraper
cardTeaser: 200K+ ASINs indexed with daily price tracking. Found 3 niches to expand into.
summary: FBA seller needed competitor data across 15 categories with no bulk export from Amazon.
challenge: Checking 50 products/day by hand — data stale before finishing a category.
solution: Custom scraper with proxy rotation pulling price, rating, BSR daily into PostgreSQL.
results: 200K+ products in 3 weeks. Launched products now top-10 in category.
context: An FBA seller in Florida tracked competitor products across 15 Amazon categories — checking prices, BSR ranks, and review counts by hand, 50 products per day at most.
By the time one category was finished, data from the first categories was already stale. Launch decisions were based on week-old snapshots, not live market conditions.
They needed an automated pipeline that indexes 200K+ ASINs, refreshes daily, and surfaces niche opportunities before competitors move in.
challengeParagraphs: Manual checks capped at 50 SKUs/day. Data stale before one full pass completed.
solutionParagraphs: Automated pipeline with daily BSR and price history across 200K+ ASINs.
resultsParagraphs: 200K+ products indexed in three weeks. Data-driven launch strategy with top-10 category ranks.

painPoints.label: Stale data before finishing one category
No BSR trend visibility over time
Capped at 50 SKUs/day manually
Profitable niches spotted too late

approach: Pilot scraper on one category to validate proxy rotation and rate limits.
Built proxy rotation pool tuned for Amazon request patterns.
PostgreSQL warehouse storing daily BSR, price, and review snapshots.
Category dashboards with niche alert rules — low competition + rising demand.
Export API for integration with existing repricing and listing tools.
Scaled from 1 to 15 categories over three weeks with zero downtime.

timeline.detail: One category proof — proxy tuning, schema design.
15 categories live, 200K+ ASINs indexed.
Niche alert rules, export API, dashboard handoff.

outcomes.label: Category rank
outcomes.detail: On new product launches informed by data.

outcomes.label: Niches found
outcomes.detail: Opportunities invisible to manual checking.

deepSections.title: Manual research hitting a ceiling
paragraphs: The seller checked 50 products per day across 15 categories — prices, BSR ranks, review velocity. By the time category 15 was done, category 1 data was a week old.
Launch decisions were gut-feel backed by stale spreadsheets. Three profitable niches were spotted months after competitors had already entered and dominated.

deepSections.title: Automated intelligence at scale
paragraphs: We built a Scrapy pipeline with proxy rotation tuned for Amazon, storing daily snapshots in PostgreSQL — price, BSR, review count, and seller count per ASIN.
Category dashboards surface niches matching alert rules: low competition, rising demand, stable pricing. An export API plugs into the seller's existing repricing tools.
Scaled from one pilot category to 15 live categories in three weeks — 200K+ ASINs indexed with daily refresh.

deepSections.title: Data-driven launches
paragraphs: The seller found three niches they would never have spotted manually — low-competition categories with rising search volume. Two new launches hit top 10 BSR within 60 days.
Research time dropped from 3+ hours daily to a 15-minute dashboard review. Every launch decision now starts with live data, not week-old spreadsheets.

deliverables.title: Amazon scraper fleet
deliverables.detail: 15 categories, 200K+ ASINs, daily refresh with proxy rotation.

deliverables.title: Historical BSR warehouse
deliverables.detail: PostgreSQL storing daily price, rank, and review snapshots for trend analysis.

deliverables.title: Category dashboards
deliverables.detail: Filter by niche, competition level, and demand signals.

deliverables.title: Export API
deliverables.detail: JSON endpoints for existing repricing and listing tools.

title: WhatsApp Order Bot
cardTeaser: 40+ unread WhatsApps at peak. Bot cut errors 90%, orders take 90 seconds.
summary: Cloud kitchen took orders via messy WhatsApp messages — wrong items, missing addresses.
challenge: Staff spent more time clarifying orders than cooking during rush hours.
solution: Structured bot: menu → cart → address → Stripe payment link, all in chat.
results: Errors down 90%. Same staff handles 2x volume.
context: A Dubai cloud kitchen took 90% of orders through WhatsApp during peak hours — unstructured messages like "same as last time" and "the usual chicken" flooded the inbox.
Staff spent more time clarifying orders than cooking. Wrong items, missing addresses, and unpaid orders were daily problems. Average order processing took 12 minutes of back-and-forth.
They needed a structured ordering flow inside WhatsApp — menu browsing, cart, address capture, and payment — without forcing customers to download a new app.
challengeParagraphs: Staff clarified orders more than they cooked. Unstructured messages caused daily errors.
solutionParagraphs: Structured WhatsApp bot with menu flow, payment, and kitchen dashboard.
resultsParagraphs: Errors down 90%. Same team handles double volume. Order time 12 min → 90s.

painPoints.label: 40+ unread messages at rush hour
Wrong items and missing addresses daily
Staff clarified more than they cooked
12-minute average order processing

approach: Mapped peak-hour order flow — identified 6 clarification points causing delays.
Designed structured menu flow in WhatsApp Business API with category browsing.
Cart → address capture → Stripe payment link — full checkout in chat.
Kitchen dashboard showing confirmed orders with prep timers and item details.
Multi-language support — English and Arabic with auto-detect.
Order status updates pushed back to customer via WhatsApp automatically.

timeline.detail: Flow mapping, menu structure, kitchen workflow.
WhatsApp bot, Stripe integration, kitchen dashboard.
Soft launch during off-peak, full rollout before Ramadan rush.

outcomes.label: Order volume
outcomes.detail: Same staff count — less clarification overhead.

outcomes.label: Error reduction
outcomes.detail: Structured flow eliminated ambiguity.

outcomes.label: Order time
outcomes.detail: Down from 12-minute average back-and-forth.

deepSections.title: Peak-hour chaos in the inbox
paragraphs: During lunch and dinner rush, the kitchen's WhatsApp inbox hit 40+ unread messages. Customers sent unstructured orders — "same as yesterday", "the usual", "2 chicken shawarma" with no address or payment confirmation.

Staff interrupted cooking constantly to clarify items, ask for addresses, and chase payments. Wrong orders went out daily. The average order took 12 minutes of back-and-forth before the kitchen could start prep.

deepSections.title: Structured ordering inside WhatsApp
paragraphs: We mapped the six clarification points causing delays and replaced them with a structured WhatsApp Business API flow: category browsing → item selection → cart review → address capture → Stripe payment link.

Confirmed orders appear on a kitchen dashboard with prep timers and full item details. Status updates — confirmed, preparing, ready — push back to the customer automatically in WhatsApp. English and Arabic supported with auto-detect.

deepSections.title: Same team, double the throughput
paragraphs: Order errors dropped 90%. Average order time fell from 12 minutes to 90 seconds. The same kitchen staff now handles double the volume because clarification overhead disappeared.
Launched before Ramadan rush — the busiest season — and held without adding headcount.

deliverables.title: WhatsApp ordering bot
deliverables.detail: Structured menu flow — browse, cart, address, pay — all inside WhatsApp.

deliverables.title: Stripe payment links
deliverables.detail: Secure checkout sent in-chat after order confirmation.

deliverables.title: Kitchen order dashboard
deliverables.detail: Confirmed orders with prep timers, item details, and status updates.

deliverables.title: Auto status updates
deliverables.detail: Order confirmed, preparing, ready — pushed back to customer in WhatsApp.

title: Law Firm Website
cardTeaser: Prospects almost did not call because the site did not match the firm.
summary: 15 years of wins but a 2008 template site hurt credibility on intake calls.
challenge: Thin attorney bios, broken mobile layout, unencrypted contact form.
solution: Professional redesign with credentials, practice area detail, and encrypted intake.
context: A Chicago boutique litigation firm had 15 years of courtroom wins but a website stuck in 2008 — Times New Roman, unencrypted contact form, no attorney profiles.
Intake calls revealed a trust gap: prospects asked "are you still practicing?" because the digital presence did not match the firm's courtroom reputation.
They needed a professional site that communicates credibility in five seconds — credentials, practice areas, and a secure intake path.
challengeParagraphs: Intake calls revealed trust gap from website. Digital presence undermined courtroom reputation.
solutionParagraphs: Professional redesign with attorney credentials, practice depth, and encrypted intake.
resultsParagraphs: Consultation requests up 55% in first quarter. Trust gap closed within 30 days.

painPoints.label: Site did not match firm reputation
Unencrypted contact form scared prospects
No attorney profiles or credentials visible
Practice area pages lacked depth

approach: Attorney interviews — collected credentials, bar admissions, notable cases.
Practice area depth pages with plain-English explanations of legal process.
Encrypted intake forms with HIPAA-aware field handling.
Mobile-first premium typography — serif headings, clean sans body.
Trust blocks: bar admissions, court admissions, professional associations.
Compliance review before launch — no client testimonials without consent.

timeline.detail: Attorney bios, practice area copy, case summaries.
Premium UI, mobile prototypes, trust block layout.
Build, compliance review, encrypted forms, go-live.

outcomes.label: Consultation requests
outcomes.detail: Q1 after launch vs prior year.

outcomes.label: "Are you still practicing?" calls
outcomes.detail: Trust gap closed within 30 days.

deepSections.title: Digital credibility gap
paragraphs: The firm had won significant cases over 15 years but their website looked abandoned — Times New Roman on a white background, a contact form with no encryption indicator, and zero attorney profiles.

Intake coordinators reported that prospects frequently asked "are you still practicing?" or "is this the right firm?" on first calls. The website actively undermined the credibility built in courtrooms.

deepSections.title: Professional redesign with compliance in mind
paragraphs: We interviewed each attorney, collected bar admissions, court admissions, and notable case summaries. Practice area pages explain the legal process in plain English — what to expect, timeline, and how the firm approaches each specialty.
Encrypted intake forms replace the old contact page. Mobile-first layout with premium typography — the site communicates authority within five seconds of landing.

deepSections.title: Intake transformation
paragraphs: Consultation requests rose 55% in Q1 after launch. The "are you still practicing?" calls stopped within 30 days. Prospects now arrive at intake calls already trusting the firm's credentials.

deliverables.title: Premium firm website
deliverables.detail: Mobile-first design with serif typography and trust-first layout.

deliverables.title: Encrypted intake forms
deliverables.detail: Secure consultation request with no sensitive data in email previews.

deliverables.title: Attorney profile pages
deliverables.detail: Credentials, bar admissions, practice focus, and notable cases.

deliverables.title: Practice area depth pages
deliverables.detail: Plain-English explanations of legal process per specialty.

title: Real Estate Listing Aggregator
cardTeaser: Checked 6 listing sites daily. One dashboard + instant alerts for new deals.
summary: Investor checked 6 portals every morning — good deals gone by the time he found them.
challenge: 2 hours daily tab-hopping. Listings often under offer before he saw them.
solution: Scrapers every 2 hours, unified dashboard, SMS when criteria match.
results: 4 below-market deals in month 1 that he would have missed manually.
context: A Toronto property investor checked six listing portals every morning — Realtor.ca, Zolo, HouseSigma, and three others — copying interesting listings into a spreadsheet before work.
By the time the spreadsheet was complete, hot deals were already under offer. Speed was the competitive edge and the manual process was too slow.
They needed unified aggregation with instant SMS alerts when listings matched their criteria — price, neighborhood, property type, and days on market.
challengeParagraphs: Speed was the competitive edge — manual process too slow for Toronto market.
solutionParagraphs: Real-time aggregation across 6 portals with instant SMS alerts.
resultsParagraphs: Four below-market acquisitions in first month. Daily research time cut from 2 hours to 15 minutes.

painPoints.label: 2 hrs daily tab-hopping across 6 portals
Missed under-market listings gone by afternoon
Spreadsheet workflow with stale data
No alert when criteria matched

approach: Mapped 6 listing portals — identified common fields and deduplication keys.
Built Playwright scrapers running every 2 hours across all portals.
Unified PostgreSQL dashboard with filters: price, neighborhood, type, DOM.
Twilio SMS alerts when new listings match saved criteria.
Historical price chart per listing — spot motivated sellers and price drops.
Mobile-friendly dashboard for on-the-go review between site visits.

timeline.detail: Scrapers, deduplication, unified dashboard.
SMS rules, criteria matching, mobile UI.

outcomes.label: Deals in month 1
outcomes.detail: Below-market acquisitions that would have been missed.

outcomes.label: Daily time saved
outcomes.detail: Tab-hopping replaced by dashboard review.

deepSections.title: Speed was the only edge
paragraphs: The investor checked six portals every morning before work — copying listings into a spreadsheet, cross-referencing prices, and flagging interesting properties. The routine took two hours and produced stale data by noon.

Below-market deals in Toronto move fast. By the time a listing appeared in the morning spreadsheet, it was often under offer by afternoon. The investor needed to know the moment a matching property hit the market.

deepSections.title: Real-time aggregation with instant alerts
paragraphs: We built Playwright scrapers for all six portals, running every two hours with deduplication across sources. Listings land in a unified PostgreSQL dashboard with filters for price, neighborhood, property type, and days on market.
Twilio SMS alerts fire the moment a new listing matches saved criteria. Each listing includes a historical price chart to spot motivated sellers and recent drops.

deepSections.title: Four deals in the first month
paragraphs: The investor closed four below-market acquisitions in month one — deals they would have missed with the manual morning routine. One alert came through at lunch; they viewed the property and submitted an offer the same afternoon.
Daily research time dropped from two hours to a 15-minute dashboard scan. Speed is now automated, not manual.

deliverables.title: Multi-portal scraper fleet
deliverables.detail: 6 listing sites scraped every 2 hours with deduplication.

deliverables.title: Unified deal dashboard
deliverables.detail: Filter by price, neighborhood, type, and days on market.

deliverables.title: SMS instant alerts
deliverables.detail: Twilio notifications when new listings match saved criteria.

deliverables.title: Price history charts
deliverables.detail: Per-listing trend lines to spot motivated sellers and drops.
